region_of_origin: Middle East / South Asia (widely consumed globally)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chickpea food product class; no specific deep-fried salted chickpea class exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated deep-fried salted chickpea code. A00LC (chickpeas) used as the closest defensible anchor with preparation qualifier noted.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical snack serving of deep-fried salted chickpeas is approximately 30 g (about a small handful), providing roughly 128 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Deep-fried chickpea, salted — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for deep-fried chickpea, salted (cr_id F09010007). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — chickpeas, boiled (Cicer arietinum); analogous estimate applied to deep-fried preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 28 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for chickpeas (Cicer arietinum). Deep-frying does not substantially raise the GI of legumes due to their resistant starch and fibre matrix; the GI value of 28 is applied as a defensible analogous estimate for the deep-fried salted preparation. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (28 × 60.4 × 0.30) / 100 = 5.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 414 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg threshold for hypertension-safe snacks
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 25–30 g per serving to keep sodium intake below 125 mg per snack occasion, and should prefer unsalted roasted chickpeas as a lower-sodium alternative with equivalent fibre and protein benefits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI of 28 combined with exceptional dietary fibre (20 g/100 g) and high protein (19.4 g/100 g) strongly moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Deep-fried salted chickpeas have a low glycemic index of 28 and high fibre content that blunts glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume moderate portions (30 g) while monitoring total carbohydrate intake, and should prefer baked or roasted preparations to reduce fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds significant fat and the sodium level of 414 mg/100 g raises cardiovascular concern, partially offset by high dietary fibre (20 g/100 g) which supports LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 25–30 g and choose unsalted, baked, or air-fried chickpea preparations to reduce sodium and fat intake while retaining the cardioprotective benefits of legume fibre and plant protein.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium at 414 mg/100 g also exceeds the RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chickpeas due to AGE formation from high-heat oil cooking; boiled, roasted, or air-fried chickpeas are strongly preferred to retain anti-inflammatory legume benefits without the pro-inflammatory AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-fried salted chickpeas are a crunchy, shelf-stable snack food made by immersing dried chickpeas (Cicer arietinum, also known as garbanzo beans) in hot oil until they achieve a hard, crispy texture, then seasoning with salt. The result is a dense, golden-brown snack with a satisfying crunch and a savoury, slightly nutty flavour. Per 100 g, this food provides 428 kcal, 19.4 g protein, 12.1 g fat, 60.4 g carbohydrate, 20.0 g dietary fibre, and 414 mg sodium. The exceptionally high dietary fibre content (20 g/100 g) is a standout nutritional feature, supporting gut health, satiety, and glycaemic moderation. The glycemic index is low at 28, making this a relatively glucose-friendly snack despite its carbohydrate density, provided portions are controlled. Clinically, the high sodium content (414 mg/100 g) is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and the deep-frying method generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI and high fibre make this a manageable snack in small portions. This food is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary patterns. It is commonly consumed as a snack and is widely available across South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Southeast Asian food cultures.
common_user_queries: Is salted chickpea snack good for diabetics?
Can I eat fried chickpeas if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09010007) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is an exact match from a primary approved regional source. GI value of 28 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for chickpeas and applied as a defensible analogous estimate for the deep-fried preparation.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried salted chickpea preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F09010007) or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central for an exact equivalent. Calcium (99 mg/100 g) and iron (3.1 mg/100 g) are reported by HPB and included. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas; deep-frying does not substantially alter legume GI due to resistant starch and fibre matrix. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (28 × 60.4 × 0.30) / 100 = 5.1. This food is not a Singaporean dish and therefore multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply; local_names fields are null. No FoodOn exact URI or SNOMED CT code exists for deep-fried salted chickpea; closest defensible anchors used with gap notes documented.
